metadata.summary: Benchmarking and quality evaluation for embedding systems: Recall@K, MRR, nDCG, A/B testing in production, evaluation dataset design, and model comparison methodology.

content.title: Embedding Quality Evaluation and Benchmarking
content.summary: MTEB scores are general benchmarks — always benchmark on your specific data. Create an evaluation set of 100-500 real query-document pairs, test at least 3-4 candidate models, measure Recall@K and MRR, then run an A/B test in production before full rollout. Without a domain-specific evaluation, model selection is guesswork.

why.p: MTEB benchmark scores do not predict real-world performance on domain-specific corpora. A model ranking lower on MTEB may outperform a higher-ranked model on your specific data because the training distribution aligns better. Legal, medical, code, and enterprise corpora frequently diverge from MTEB results by 5-15%. Domain-specific evaluation is the only reliable selection signal.

list.item: Selecting an embedding model for a new project (always).
Evaluating whether to switch from one model to another.
Diagnosing poor retrieval quality in production.
Validating a model update before re-embedding the full corpus.
Prototype with fewer than 50 documents — use any reasonable model and defer evaluation.
When you cannot create a query-document evaluation set — fall back to MTEB scores with domain-awareness caveats.

section.name: evaluation-dataset-design
section.p: Create an evaluation dataset before benchmarking models:

list.item: Minimum 100 query-document pairs; 500 preferred for statistical significance.
Queries must be representative of real usage — draw from query logs if available.
Documents must come from the actual corpus, not synthetic content.
Include relevance labels (binary or graded) from domain experts.
Include edge cases: short queries, long queries, multilingual, rare entities, acronyms.
No data leakage — queries must not appear in model training data.
Balanced distribution of relevance — avoid datasets that are all positives.
Recall@1, @5, @10, @100: fraction of relevant documents retrieved in top K. Primary metric for RAG — aim for Recall@10 above 0.7.
MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank): average of 1/rank for the first relevant result. Primary metric for search ranking. Aim for MRR above 0.5.
Precision@K: fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant at rank K.
nDCG@10 (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain): accounts for position and graded relevance. Most complete single metric for ranked retrieval.

example.title: Recall@K and MRR calculation

list.item: Step 1: Create evaluation set — 100-500 real query-document pairs with binary relevance labels.
Step 2: Shortlist 3-5 candidate models based on MTEB scores and use case fit.
Step 3: For each model, embed the evaluation corpus and queries.
Step 4: Measure Recall@1, @5, @10, MRR, and latency p50/p95 for each model.
Step 5: Calculate cost per 1M tokens at your expected monthly volume.
Step 6: Select the model with the best quality/cost/latency trade-off for your requirements.
Step 7: Document the decision and trade-offs accepted.

section.name: model-selection-checklist
section.p: Model selection requirements to document before benchmarking:

list.item: Primary use case: semantic search / RAG / clustering / classification / similarity / anomaly detection.
Budget per month in USD.
Latency requirement in ms (p95).
Max document length in tokens.
Languages required.
Privacy requirements: cloud API allowed or on-prem/air-gapped only.
GPU availability.
Target Recall@10 percentage.
Target MRR value.
Acceptable quality loss for cost savings.

section.name: ab-testing-production
section.p: A/B testing a new model in production:

list.item: Control group: current model, treatment group: new model, random user assignment.
Calculate required sample size upfront for statistical power (p-value below 0.05, confidence interval calculated).
Measure business metrics: click-through rate (CTR), user satisfaction, task completion rate, time to find relevant result.
Define early stopping rules to catch regressions before full rollout.
Rollback plan: keep old vectors until new model passes A/B gates.
Run for at least one full week to capture weekly query patterns.

section.name: failure-analysis
section.p: When retrieval quality is below target:

list.item: False negatives (relevant docs not retrieved): examine chunk size — try 256-512 tokens for Q&A, 1000-2000 for summarization.
False positives (irrelevant docs ranked high): consider adding hybrid search (BM25) to penalize semantically similar but lexically different content.
Pattern identification in failures: group failures by query type, document type, length, or vocabulary to identify systematic gaps.
Root cause analysis: check if failures cluster around specific topics suggesting domain shift — consider fine-tuning or domain-specific model.

section.name: evaluation-prompts
section.prompt:
I need to select an embedding model for my project. Help me choose the best option.
Use Case: [semantic search / RAG / classification / clustering / etc.] Requirements: - Expected query volume: [queries/day] - Document corpus size: [number of documents] - Average document length: [tokens] - Languages needed: [list] - Maximum acceptable latency: [ms] - Monthly budget: [$] - Privacy constraints: [cloud API allowed / on-prem only] Current Setup: - Vector database: [Qdrant/Pinecone/pgvector/etc.] - Tech stack: [Python/Node.js/etc.]
Please recommend: 1. Primary model choice with rationale 2. Alternative option for comparison 3. Dimension recommendation 4. Expected cost estimate 5. Implementation considerations

Help me benchmark multiple embedding models for my use case.
Models to Compare: 1. [Model 1] 2. [Model 2] 3. [Model 3]
Evaluation Criteria: - Quality: [metrics to measure] - Speed: [latency, throughput] - Cost: [per token, monthly estimate]
Test Conditions: - Test dataset size: [count] - Hardware: [specs] - Batch sizes to test: [list]
Please provide: 1. Comprehensive evaluation plan 2. Metrics to capture 3. Statistical significance requirements 4. Visualization recommendations 5. Decision framework for final selection

Help me create an evaluation dataset for my embedding system.
System Purpose: [describe] Available Resources: - Existing queries: [yes/no, count] - Query logs: [yes/no] - User feedback: [yes/no] - Domain experts: [yes/no]
Corpus: [document count, types, topics] Evaluation Goals: [Recall@K / MRR / nDCG, edge cases]
Please help me: 1. Design the evaluation dataset structure 2. Determine optimal dataset size 3. Create query generation strategy 4. Define relevance labeling guidelines 5. Handle edge cases and hard negatives 6. Establish baseline expectations

list.item: Weekly: review error rates, check cache hit rates, monitor cost trends.
Monthly: evaluate new model releases, review quality metrics, optimize cost if needed, rotate API keys.
Quarterly: benchmark against new models, review and update evaluation dataset, assess fine-tuning opportunities, review and update documentation.
Benchmark new model on your evaluation data.
Test in staging environment with shadow traffic.
Prepare rollback plan (keep old index until new one passes quality gates).
Communicate change to stakeholders.
After update: re-embed all documents if dimensions changed, monitor quality metrics closely, watch for anomalies in user behavior.

checklist.check: Evaluation dataset has at least 100 real query-document pairs.
Queries drawn from actual usage patterns, not synthetic generation.
At least 3 candidate models benchmarked before selection.
Recall@10, MRR, and latency p50/p95 measured for each candidate.
Cost per 1M tokens calculated at expected monthly volume.
A/B test plan defined before production rollout.
Statistical significance (p-value below 0.05) required for A/B decisions.
Rollback plan documented before model update.

antipattern.description: Using synthetic benchmarks (LLM-generated query-document pairs) as the primary evaluation signal.
antipattern.reason: Synthetic queries match model training distributions and produce inflated recall numbers. Real user queries contain typos, ambiguity, and domain jargon that synthetic queries omit. Always validate with real queries.

antipattern.description: Comparing models only on latency and cost, skipping quality measurement.
antipattern.reason: A 10x cheaper model with 20% lower recall can cost more in business terms (missed results, user drop-off) than the savings. Always measure quality first, then optimize within the quality constraint.
